1.synthesis: In the second year of his reign, Nebuchadnezzar is troubled by dreams and cannot sleep (v.1). He summons the magicians, enchanters, sorcerers, and Chaldeans, but demands the impossible: that they tell him both the dream and its interpretation, on pain of death (vv.2–9). The Chaldeans protest that “there is not a man on earth who can meet the king’s demand,” for only the gods could reveal it, “whose dwelling is not with flesh” (vv.10–11). Enraged, the king decrees that all the wise men of Babylon be destroyed, and they seek Daniel and his companions to kill them (vv.12–13). The integrative thread is the bankruptcy of pagan wisdom before the secrets of God: the king’s impossible demand exposes that Babylon’s sages, for all their arts, cannot reach the gods “whose dwelling is not with flesh” — and the death sentence that ensnares even Daniel sets the stage for the living God to reveal what no human wisdom can, vindicating himself as the only true revealer of mysteries.
1.historical_context: The king’s demand that the sages recover the dream itself, not merely interpret it (vv.5–9), was a test to expose charlatans, since any clever interpreter could spin meaning from a known dream. The Chaldeans’ admission that only the gods, “whose dwelling is not with flesh,” could reveal it (v.11) unwittingly testifies to the gulf between humanity and the divine that the God of heaven will bridge. The threatened mass execution of the wise men (vv.12–13) reflects the absolute and arbitrary power of an ancient despot.
1.literary_structure: This opening section (vv.1–13) sets up the great dream narrative by establishing the impossible demand and the failure of Babylon’s wisdom (vv.10–11). The death decree (v.13) draws Daniel into the crisis, creating the tension that God’s revelation will resolve, and contrasting human helplessness with the divine disclosure to come.
1.theological_application: Human wisdom, however refined, cannot penetrate the secret counsels of God (vv.10–11; 1 Corinthians 2:11). The Chaldeans’ confession that the gods do not dwell with flesh exposes the longing that only revelation — and ultimately incarnation — can answer. The believer is reminded that the deep things of God are known only as he reveals them (1 Corinthians 2:10), and that the crises which threaten God’s servants often become the occasions for his power and truth to be displayed.
1.christology: The Chaldeans’ despairing admission that the gods’ “dwelling is not with flesh” (v.11) is answered in the incarnation, when the Word became flesh and dwelt among us (John 1:14), God himself taking up residence with humanity. The God of heaven who alone reveals mysteries (Daniel 2:28) has spoken finally in his Son (Hebrews 1:1–2), in whom the hidden wisdom of God is made known. Where pagan wisdom failed and stood condemned to death, Christ is the revelation that brings life.

14.synthesis: Daniel responds with prudence and discretion, learning the cause and asking the king for time (vv.14–16). He gathers Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah to “seek mercy from the God of heaven concerning this mystery,” and the mystery is revealed to Daniel in a night vision (vv.17–19). He blesses God: “Blessed be the name of God forever and ever, to whom belong wisdom and might… he changes times and seasons; he removes kings and sets up kings… he reveals deep and hidden things” (vv.20–23). Brought before the king, Daniel disclaims all personal wisdom: “there is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries” (vv.24–30). The integrative thread is believing prayer and God-glorifying humility as the path to revelation: faced with death, Daniel turns first to prayer with his friends, and when the mystery is given, he refuses any credit — ascribing all wisdom, might, and the disclosure itself to the God of heaven who rules history and reveals what is hidden.
14.historical_context: Daniel’s prudent approach to Arioch and the king (vv.14–16) shows wisdom under threat. The communal prayer for “mercy from the God of heaven” (v.18) and the night-vision revelation (v.19) model dependence on God rather than divination. Daniel’s doxology (vv.20–23) confesses God as the sovereign who “removes kings and sets up kings,” the book’s central theme. His insistence “there is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries” (v.28) directly counters the Chaldeans’ claim that no such revelation was possible (Daniel 2:11).
14.literary_structure: This section (vv.14–30) moves from crisis to prayer to praise, the doxology (vv.20–23) standing at its heart as the theological key. Daniel’s repeated deflection of credit to “a God in heaven” (v.28) frames the dream’s interpretation that follows, ensuring God, not Daniel, receives the glory for the revelation.
14.theological_application: When faced with the impossible, the believer’s first recourse is believing, communal prayer for mercy (vv.17–18; Philippians 4:6). Daniel’s doxology teaches us to praise God as the sovereign Lord of history and the revealer of hidden things (vv.21–22), and his refusal of personal credit models the humility that gives God all glory for whatever wisdom or success we are granted (v.30; 1 Corinthians 4:7). Answered prayer should always return to God as praise.
14.christology: Daniel, the intercessor who seeks mercy and to whom the saving mystery is revealed (vv.18–19), foreshadows Christ, the true Mediator through whom “the mystery hidden for ages” is now disclosed (Colossians 1:26–27; Ephesians 3:9). The God who “changes times and seasons” and reveals deep things (vv.21–22) has spoken the final revelation in his Son, in whom the hidden purpose of the ages stands unveiled, and who intercedes for his people that they may receive wisdom from above (James 1:5).

31.synthesis: Daniel describes the great image: a head of gold, chest and arms of silver, middle and thighs of bronze, legs of iron, and feet of iron mixed with clay (vv.31–33). “A stone was cut out by no human hand,” struck the image on its feet, and broke it to pieces, while the stone “became a great mountain and filled the whole earth” (vv.34–35). Daniel interprets: Nebuchadnezzar is the head of gold, followed by inferior kingdoms, until “the God of heaven will set up a kingdom that shall never be destroyed… it shall stand forever” (vv.36–45). The integrative thread is the rise and fall of human empires answered by the everlasting kingdom of God: the dazzling statue of successive kingdoms, however imposing, is shattered by a stone cut without human hands — God’s own kingdom — which alone endures and fills the earth, declaring that all the kingdoms of men are passing while the kingdom God establishes will never end.
31.historical_context: The four-metal image (vv.32–33) represents a succession of empires, traditionally Babylon (gold), Medo-Persia (silver), Greece (bronze), and Rome (iron), with the decreasing value of the metals signaling declining glory. The “stone cut without hands” that becomes a mountain (vv.34–35) is the kingdom of God, established in the days of those kings (v.44). The motif of a divinely given kingdom that outlasts and supplants human empires runs throughout Daniel and the prophets (Isaiah 2:2).
31.literary_structure: This central section (vv.31–45) presents the dream and its interpretation, the four-part statue (vv.32–33) contrasted with the stone-become-mountain (v.35). The climactic interpretation that God’s kingdom “shall never be destroyed” (v.44) is the theological summit, establishing the book’s vision of history moving toward the everlasting reign of God.
31.theological_application: Earthly kingdoms, however golden, are transient and will be shattered, while the kingdom God establishes endures forever (v.44; Hebrews 12:28). The believer’s hope and allegiance belong to this unshakable kingdom, not to the rising and falling powers of the age. The stone “cut without hands” teaches that God’s kingdom comes by his own act, not human achievement, and its growth into a mountain filling the earth assures us that God’s reign will finally triumph over all rival dominions.
31.christology: The stone cut without human hands that crushes the kingdoms and fills the earth (vv.34–35) is Christ and his kingdom — the stone the builders rejected, made the cornerstone, which falls in judgment and grows to fill the world (Matthew 21:42–44; Luke 20:18). His kingdom, inaugurated “in the days of those kings” (v.44) and consummated at his return, “shall never be destroyed” (Luke 1:33). All empires give way to the everlasting dominion of the Son of Man (Daniel 7:14).

46.synthesis: Nebuchadnezzar falls on his face, pays homage to Daniel, and declares, “Truly, your God is God of gods and Lord of kings, and a revealer of mysteries, for you have been able to reveal this mystery” (vv.46–47). He makes Daniel great, sets him over the whole province of Babylon and over all its wise men (v.48), and at Daniel’s request appoints Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego over the affairs of the province, while Daniel remains at the king’s court (v.49). The integrative thread is the exaltation of God’s servant and the confession of God’s supremacy by a pagan king: the revelation vindicates the God of heaven before the most powerful man on earth, who confesses him “God of gods and Lord of kings,” and Daniel is elevated to high office — the faithful exile raised to influence, and his friends with him, as God begins to fulfill his purpose through them in the heart of the empire.
46.historical_context: Nebuchadnezzar’s homage to Daniel (v.46) and his confession of Daniel’s God as “God of gods and Lord of kings” (v.47) is a remarkable acknowledgment, though not yet personal conversion. Daniel’s elevation over the province and the wise men (v.48) parallels Joseph’s rise in Egypt (Genesis 41:40–41). His securing posts for his three friends (v.49) sets up the confrontation of chapter 3, where their high profile makes them targets.
46.literary_structure: This closing section (vv.46–49) resolves the chapter with the king’s confession (v.47) and Daniel’s exaltation (v.48). The placement of the three friends in office (v.49) provides the narrative bridge to the fiery-furnace account, while the pagan king’s praise reinforces the book’s theme of God’s supremacy over the nations.
46.theological_application: God vindicates his faithful servants and brings even pagan rulers to confess his supremacy (v.47; Philippians 2:10–11). The believer is encouraged that God can elevate his people to positions of influence for his purposes, as he did Daniel and Joseph, and that faithful witness may move others — even the powerful — toward acknowledging the true God. Daniel’s remembering his friends in his promotion (v.49) models using influence for the good of fellow believers rather than self alone.
46.christology: Daniel’s exaltation after revealing the mystery, and the king’s homage before him (vv.46–48), foreshadows the exaltation of Christ, who, having made known the saving mystery and accomplished it, is given the name above every name, before whom every knee will bow and every tongue confess him Lord (Philippians 2:9–11). The pagan king’s confession that Daniel’s God is “Lord of kings” (v.47) anticipates the day when the King of kings is universally acknowledged (Revelation 19:16).
